registry.description: Patience is the inner quality of continuing to wait and trust without being unravelled by delay or difficulty. The Hebrew vocabulary here includes silence, the stilling of inner turmoil in the face of what is hard; waiting in the sense of active, expectant trust; and the weariness that must be borne without giving up. The Greek adds the sense of holding back under pressure. Patience is not passive but active: it is the continuous choice to keep trusting while the situation remains unresolved. It is counted among the fruits of the Spirit and the character of God.

cross_registry_links.note: aph (H0639) is the primary anger term in the OT and is the constituent element of the compound arek aph (slow to anger). The patience word set necessarily includes the full anger term set because patience is defined in OT as the restraint or lengthening of anger.
cross_registry_links.link_type_description: The same Hebrew or Greek term appears in both word studies

cross_registry_links.note: la.ah (H3811) meaning 'to be weary, impatient' is the negative of patience and is semantically contrastive with the ARK root family. Jer 15:6 attributes la.ah to God ('I am weary of relenting'), creating a parallel with the god_as_subject instances in the ARK family.
cross_registry_links.link_type_description: The two registries share overlapping meaning space

cross_registry_links.note: ya.chal (H3176) carries both the waiting and hoping senses and will likely overlap with any hope word set. The related word to.che.let (hope) is a derivative.
cross_registry_links.link_type_description: The registries share a common etymological root

cross_registry_links.note: Eph 4:2 appears in both the humility word set (tapeinofrosunē) and the patience word set (makrothumia): 'with all humility and gentleness, with patience, bearing with one another in love.' Col 3:12 similarly co-lists humility and patience.
